quiz_questions.question_text: What is a biological community?

options.text: All the populations of different species living and interacting in a particular area
All the living and non-living components of an area combined
The physical environment where organisms live
All the individuals of one species in an area

quiz_questions.explanation: A biological community consists of all the populations of different species living and interacting in a particular area at the same time. Unlike a population (one species) or an ecosystem (biotic + abiotic), a community focuses specifically on the interacting species. Community ecology studies how these species interact through competition, predation, symbiosis, and other relationships.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is species richness?

options.text: The total number of individual organisms in a community
How evenly distributed individuals are among species
The total number of different species present in a community
The total biomass of all organisms in a community

quiz_questions.explanation: Species richness is simply the count of different species present in a community. A tropical rainforest with 500 tree species has higher species richness than a temperate forest with 50 tree species. Species richness is one component of species diversity but does not account for how evenly individuals are distributed among those species.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does species diversity differ from species richness?

options.text: Species diversity only measures the rarest species in a community
Species diversity counts individual organisms while richness counts species
Species diversity combines both the number of species (richness) and the relative abundance of each species (evenness)
Species diversity and species richness mean exactly the same thing

quiz_questions.explanation: Species diversity is a more comprehensive measure than species richness because it combines two components: (1) species richness — the number of different species, and (2) species evenness — how equally individuals are distributed among those species. A community with 10 species equally represented has higher diversity than one with 10 species where 90% belong to one species.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a keystone species?

options.text: Any species at the top of the food chain
A species that creates physical habitat structure for others
A species that has a disproportionately large effect on its community relative to its abundance
The most abundant species in a community

quiz_questions.explanation: A keystone species exerts an influence on its community that is disproportionately large relative to its abundance or biomass. Removing a keystone species causes dramatic changes in community structure. The classic example is the sea star Pisaster in Pacific tide pools — its removal allows mussels to dominate, reducing species diversity from 15+ species to just a few.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a foundation species?

options.text: A species whose presence indicates environmental health
The first species to colonize a new habitat during succession
The species with the largest population in a community
A species that creates or significantly modifies habitat, enabling many other species to exist in the community

quiz_questions.explanation: A foundation species creates or significantly modifies the physical habitat, enabling many other species to exist in the community. Unlike keystone species (influence disproportionate to abundance), foundation species typically have high abundance and physically structure the environment. Examples include coral (building reef structure), kelp (creating underwater forests), and large trees (forming forest canopy).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an indicator species?

options.text: The first species to arrive in a new habitat
A species that always has the largest population
A species whose presence, absence, or health reflects specific environmental conditions in the community
A species that indicates the direction of ecological succession

quiz_questions.explanation: An indicator species is one whose presence, absence, or health reflects specific environmental conditions in the community. Scientists monitor indicator species to assess ecosystem health. For example, amphibians are indicators of water quality (they absorb pollutants through their skin), and lichens indicate air quality.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the intermediate disturbance hypothesis?

options.text: Disturbance has no effect on species diversity in communities
Species diversity is highest at moderate levels of disturbance because both competitive dominants and early colonizers can coexist
Communities with no disturbance have the highest species diversity
High disturbance levels always produce the most diverse communities

quiz_questions.explanation: The intermediate disturbance hypothesis proposes that species diversity is highest at moderate (intermediate) levels of disturbance. Too little disturbance allows competitive dominants to exclude other species; too much disturbance eliminates all but the hardiest colonizers. Moderate disturbance prevents any one species from dominating while not being so frequent that sensitive species cannot establish.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between a dominant species and a keystone species?

options.text: They are exactly the same concept with different names
A dominant species has the greatest abundance or biomass; a keystone species has disproportionate influence relative to its low abundance
A dominant species is always a predator while a keystone species is always a plant
A dominant species is rare while a keystone species is abundant

quiz_questions.explanation: A dominant species controls the community through its sheer abundance or biomass (e.g., sugar maples dominating a forest canopy). A keystone species has an effect disproportionate to its relatively low abundance (e.g., sea stars controlling mussel populations). Both influence community structure, but through different mechanisms — abundance vs. outsized ecological role.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is bottom-up control in a community?

options.text: Middle trophic level organisms control both producers and top predators
Predators at the top of the food web control population sizes of lower trophic levels
Decomposers control all other organisms in the community
Community structure is determined by nutrient availability and producer abundance at the base of the food web

quiz_questions.explanation: Bottom-up control (also called resource-driven control) means community structure is determined by factors at the base of the food web — nutrient availability, sunlight, and producer abundance. More nutrients → more producers → more herbivores → more predators. Changes ripple upward through trophic levels from the bottom.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is top-down control in a community?

options.text: Producers determine the structure of the entire community
The oldest species in a community controls the newest arrivals
Sunlight and temperature control all community interactions
Predators at higher trophic levels regulate the abundance of organisms at lower trophic levels through a trophic cascade

quiz_questions.explanation: Top-down control (predator-driven control) means that predators at higher trophic levels regulate the abundance of organisms at lower levels. This can create a trophic cascade: removing a top predator causes herbivore populations to explode, which overgrazes producers. The Yellowstone wolf reintroduction is a classic example of restoring top-down control.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes species richness?

options.text: The geographic range over which a species is found
How evenly individuals are distributed among species in a community
The total number of individual organisms living in a community
The total number of different species present in a community, without regard to their relative abundances

quiz_questions.explanation: Species richness is the total number of different species present in a community, without considering how many individuals belong to each species. It is the simplest measure of biodiversity — simply a count of species. A coral reef with 1,000 species has greater species richness than a tundra with 50 species.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes species diversity?

options.text: The total number of species in a community regardless of abundance
The variety of ecosystems across a geographic region
A measure that combines species richness (number of species) and species evenness (relative abundance of each species) in a community
The genetic variation within a single species' population

quiz_questions.explanation: Species diversity is a composite measure that combines two components: species richness (how many species are present) and species evenness (how equally individuals are distributed among those species). A community with many species that are roughly equal in abundance has higher diversity than one with many species but dominated by just one or two.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a keystone species?

options.text: A species that exerts a disproportionately large influence on community structure relative to its abundance or biomass
The most abundant or dominant species in a community
A species that arrives first during ecological succession
A species that physically constructs the habitat for the community

quiz_questions.explanation: A keystone species is one that exerts a disproportionately large influence on community structure and function relative to its abundance or biomass. The name comes from the keystone of an arch — a small stone that holds the entire structure together. Removing a keystone species causes dramatic changes in community composition. Classic examples include sea otters, wolves, and the sea star Pisaster.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an indicator species?

options.text: A species whose presence, absence, or health serves as a measure of environmental conditions or ecosystem health
The most visible or noticeable species in a community
A species that indicates the direction of prevailing winds in an area
A species used to mark territorial boundaries between communities

quiz_questions.explanation: An indicator species is one whose presence, absence, or population health serves as a measure of environmental conditions or ecosystem health. Scientists monitor these species to detect changes in habitat quality, pollution levels, or climate. Examples include amphibians (sensitive to water quality), lichens (air quality indicators), and trout (clean, cold water indicators).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a foundation species?

options.text: A rare species that has minimal impact on its community
The first species to colonize a new habitat during primary succession
A species that creates or significantly modifies the physical habitat, enabling many other species to live in the community
A species at the base of the food chain that provides energy to all other species

quiz_questions.explanation: A foundation species creates or significantly modifies the physical habitat, enabling many other species to live in the community. Foundation species are typically abundant and physically structure the environment. Examples include corals (building reef frameworks), kelp (creating undersea forests), beavers (creating wetlands with dams), and large trees (forming forest canopy and understory).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the intermediate disturbance hypothesis?

options.text: The ecological theory that species diversity is highest at moderate levels of disturbance, because neither competitive dominants nor only hardy colonizers prevail
The hypothesis that only intermediate-sized organisms survive disturbances
The idea that species diversity increases with increasingly frequent disturbances
The theory that all ecological disturbances are harmful to communities

quiz_questions.explanation: The intermediate disturbance hypothesis states that species diversity is highest at moderate (intermediate) levels of disturbance. With too little disturbance, competitively dominant species exclude others; with too much disturbance, only the hardiest colonizers survive. Moderate disturbance prevents competitive exclusion while allowing sensitive species to persist, maximizing diversity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a dominant species?

options.text: A species with a disproportionately large effect relative to its small abundance
A predator that controls all prey species in the community
The largest individual organism in a community
A species that has the highest abundance or biomass in a community and exerts major control over community structure through sheer numbers

quiz_questions.explanation: A dominant species is one that has the highest abundance or biomass in a community and exerts major control over community structure through its sheer numbers. Unlike keystone species (disproportionate effect relative to low abundance), dominant species control communities because they are the most prevalent. Examples include sugar maples in deciduous forests and grasses on prairies.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes bottom-up control?

options.text: A model of community regulation in which nutrient availability and producer abundance at the base of the food web determine the structure of higher trophic levels
The idea that organisms at the bottom of the ocean control marine communities
A model in which top predators control the abundance of all lower trophic levels
A management strategy where lower-level employees make all decisions

quiz_questions.explanation: Bottom-up control is a model of community regulation in which nutrient availability and producer abundance at the base of the food web determine community structure. Changes ripple upward: more nutrients → more producers → more herbivores → more predators. Ecosystems limited by nutrient availability (like many freshwater lakes) often show strong bottom-up control.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes top-down control?

options.text: The idea that the tallest trees in a forest control all other organisms
A system where climate is the only factor controlling community structure
A model of community regulation in which predators at higher trophic levels control the population sizes of organisms at lower trophic levels through a trophic cascade
A model in which producers at the base of the food web control all higher levels

quiz_questions.explanation: Top-down control is a model of community regulation in which predators at higher trophic levels control population sizes at lower levels through trophic cascades. Removing a top predator causes herbivore populations to explode, overgrazing producers. The classic example is otter removal leading to sea urchin explosion and kelp forest destruction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an ecological disturbance?

options.text: The gradual process of ecological succession after a stable period
The normal daily fluctuations in temperature and light in an ecosystem
An event — natural or human-caused — that disrupts community structure by changing resource availability, the physical environment, or species composition
Any interaction between two different species in a community

quiz_questions.explanation: An ecological disturbance is an event — natural or human-caused — that disrupts community structure by changing resource availability, the physical environment, or species composition. Examples include fires, floods, storms, volcanic eruptions, logging, and disease outbreaks. Disturbances can reset succession and open niches for new species.

quiz_questions.question_text: Robert Paine removed the sea star Pisaster ochraceus from tide pools along the Washington coast in the 1960s. Without the sea star, mussels (Pisaster's preferred prey) quickly dominated the rocks, crowding out barnacles, algae, limpets, and other species. Species diversity dropped from 15 species to 8. When Pisaster was present, it kept mussel populations in check, allowing many other species to coexist. What concept does this experiment BEST demonstrate?

options.text: Foundation species — Pisaster creates the physical rock habitat
Dominant species — Pisaster is the most abundant organism in the tide pool
Keystone species — Pisaster has a disproportionately large effect on community diversity relative to its abundance
Indicator species — Pisaster's health reflects environmental quality

quiz_questions.explanation: This is the original keystone species experiment. Paine's work showed that Pisaster, despite being relatively uncommon, had a disproportionately large effect on community structure. By preying on competitively dominant mussels, the sea star prevented competitive exclusion and maintained high species diversity. Its removal dramatically reduced diversity — the hallmark of a keystone species.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a tropical rainforest, researchers compare two plots with identical species richness (50 tree species each). In Plot A, each species makes up roughly 2% of all trees. In Plot B, one species comprises 80% of trees and the remaining 49 species share the other 20%. Which plot has higher species diversity, and why?

options.text: Neither plot can be compared because they are in a tropical rainforest
Both plots have identical diversity because they have the same number of species
Plot A — it has higher species evenness, so its combined diversity (richness + evenness) is greater
Plot B — having one dominant species increases diversity

quiz_questions.explanation: Plot A has higher species diversity because diversity combines both richness AND evenness. Both plots have equal species richness (50 species), but Plot A has much higher evenness (each species equally represented at ~2%). Plot B's heavy dominance by one species (80%) means most species are rare, reducing effective diversity despite equal richness.

quiz_questions.question_text: Coral reefs in the Caribbean support thousands of species by building complex three-dimensional limestone structures. Fish, invertebrates, algae, and countless other organisms live within the reef framework. When coral bleaching kills the reef-building corals, the entire community collapses even though the corals were not the predators or the most numerous individuals. What role do corals play in this community?

options.text: Dominant species — corals control the reef through predation
Indicator species — corals indicate that the water is polluted
Keystone species — corals have a disproportionate effect relative to their low abundance
Foundation species — corals create the physical habitat structure that enables thousands of other species to exist

quiz_questions.explanation: Corals are foundation species — they physically construct the reef framework (limestone skeleton) that provides the structural habitat for thousands of other species. Unlike keystone species (disproportionate effect relative to low abundance), corals are abundant and their role is specifically habitat creation. When the foundation species is lost, the physical habitat collapses and the entire community it supported disappears.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ecologists studying streams in the Pacific Northwest notice that streams near salmon runs have healthier riparian forests than streams without salmon. Bears and eagles catch salmon and carry them to the forest, where their carcasses decompose and release marine-derived nutrients (nitrogen, phosphorus) that fertilize the trees. The more nutrients available, the more the trees grow, supporting more insects, birds, and mammals. What type of community control does this illustrate?

options.text: Top-down control — bears and eagles are predators controlling the community from above
Intermediate disturbance — salmon runs create moderate disturbance levels
Competitive exclusion — salmon outcompete trees for nutrients
Bottom-up control — nutrient input (from salmon carcasses) at the base of the food web drives productivity at all higher levels

quiz_questions.explanation: This illustrates bottom-up control — nutrients from decomposing salmon carcasses increase the resource base for producers (trees), which ripples upward through trophic levels: more tree growth → more insects → more birds and mammals. The community structure is driven by nutrient availability at the base. While bears are predators, the community effect described is resource-driven (bottom-up), not predation-driven (top-down).

quiz_questions.question_text: When sea otters were hunted to near extinction along the Pacific coast, sea urchin populations (which otters normally eat) exploded. The unchecked urchins devoured kelp forests, converting lush underwater forests into barren 'urchin barrens' with very few species. After otter populations were protected and recovered, urchins declined, and kelp forests regrew with full biodiversity. What does this BEST illustrate?

options.text: Indicator species — otters indicate water quality problems
Intermediate disturbance — otter hunting was a moderate disturbance
Bottom-up control — kelp controls the urchin and otter populations
Top-down control and trophic cascade — the top predator (otter) regulates herbivores (urchins), which in turn controls producers (kelp)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a classic example of top-down control and trophic cascade. Sea otters (top predator) control sea urchin populations (herbivores), which in turn determines kelp abundance (producers). Removing the top predator caused a cascade: otters removed → urchins explode → kelp destroyed → biodiversity collapses. Restoring otters reversed the cascade, demonstrating predator-driven community regulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A study of grassland communities found that fire frequency determines species diversity. Plots that never burned became dominated by a few tall grass species. Plots burned every year supported only fire-resistant species. But plots burned every 3-5 years had the highest species diversity — both fire-tolerant and fire-sensitive species coexisted. Which concept does this BEST support?

options.text: Species richness — the plots with fire have more total individual plants
The intermediate disturbance hypothesis — moderate fire frequency prevents competitive exclusion while allowing sensitive species to persist
Top-down control — fire is a predator that controls grass populations
Foundation species — fire is a foundation species that builds habitat

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a textbook example of the intermediate disturbance hypothesis. No fire (low disturbance) → competitive dominants exclude others. Annual fire (high disturbance) → only fire-resistant species survive. Fire every 3-5 years (moderate disturbance) → prevents competitive exclusion but is infrequent enough for fire-sensitive species to establish between burns, maximizing diversity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Scientists monitoring a wetland use frogs as study organisms. They find that declining frog populations correlate with increased pesticide runoff from nearby farms. When the pesticide levels are low, frog populations are healthy and diverse. When they discover that frogs in a new wetland show deformities and declining numbers, they suspect contamination before even testing the water. What ecological role are the frogs serving?

options.text: Indicator species — frog health reflects water quality and environmental conditions in the wetland
Foundation species — frogs create the wetland habitat structure
Dominant species — frogs are the most abundant organisms in the wetland
Keystone species — frogs have a disproportionate community effect

quiz_questions.explanation: Frogs are serving as indicator species — their presence, health, and population status reflect the environmental conditions of the wetland. Amphibians are excellent indicators because they absorb substances through their permeable skin, live in both water and on land, and are sensitive to pollutants. Scientists use indicator species as early warning systems for environmental problems.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a North American deciduous forest, sugar maples make up 75% of the tree canopy, with oaks, beeches, and other species comprising the rest. The sugar maples shade the forest floor, decompose slowly to create acidic soil, and produce massive amounts of leaf litter. While sugar maples are not irreplaceable in the same way a keystone species would be, their overwhelming abundance shapes the entire forest environment. What ecological role do sugar maples play?

options.text: Indicator species — sugar maples indicate that the forest soil is acidic
Dominant species — sugar maples control the community through their high abundance and biomass, shaping environmental conditions
Keystone species — sugar maples have a disproportionate effect relative to their low numbers
Invasive species — sugar maples outcompete native trees

quiz_questions.explanation: Sugar maples are a dominant species — they control the community through their overwhelmingly high abundance and biomass (75% of canopy). They shape the forest environment by determining light availability (canopy shading), soil chemistry (acidic leaf litter), and nutrient cycling. This differs from a keystone species, which has disproportionate influence relative to LOW abundance.

quiz_questions.question_text: Lake Victoria in Africa once supported over 500 endemic cichlid fish species — the most species-rich freshwater fish community on Earth. In the 1950s, Nile perch (a large predatory fish) was introduced as a food source. Nile perch ate most of the smaller cichlid species, and eutrophication (nutrient pollution) muddied the water, disrupting visual mating signals that kept cichlid species reproductively isolated. Over 200 cichlid species went extinct. What concepts does this scenario illustrate about community ecology?

options.text: Bottom-up control — nutrient pollution drove the cichlid extinction
The intermediate disturbance hypothesis — moderate introduction of Nile perch increased diversity
Catastrophic biodiversity loss caused by the combined effects of an invasive predator (Nile perch) and pollution disrupting species interactions
Foundation species loss — cichlids built the lake habitat for other species

quiz_questions.explanation: Lake Victoria illustrates catastrophic species diversity loss from multiple human impacts acting together. The Nile perch (invasive species) directly consumed cichlids, while eutrophication (pollution) muddied the water and disrupted the visual mate recognition signals that maintained reproductive isolation between similar cichlid species. This combination destroyed the highest species richness of any freshwater fish community — showing how multiple threats compound to devastate biodiversity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Scientists compare two neighboring marine communities over 10 years. Community A, a seagrass bed, has 150 species and high evenness (many species in balanced proportions) and recovers quickly after a hurricane. Community B, an area with one dominant invasive algae covering 90% of the substrate and 40 other sparse species, takes years to recover after the same hurricane and loses 15 species permanently. What principle about biodiversity and community resilience does this comparison BEST illustrate?

options.text: Higher species diversity (richness + evenness) makes communities more resilient — able to resist and recover from disturbance more effectively
Hurricanes are beneficial because they remove invasive species
Low-diversity communities are more resilient because they have simpler food webs
Community resilience depends only on species richness, not evenness

quiz_questions.explanation: This comparison illustrates the diversity-stability hypothesis — communities with higher species diversity (both richness AND evenness) are more resilient against disturbance. Community A's high diversity provides functional redundancy (if one species is lost, others can fill its role) and balanced interactions, enabling rapid recovery. Community B's low evenness (dominated by one species) makes it fragile — if the dominant species is damaged, few alternatives exist to maintain ecosystem function.
